html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Six — Part Two

The Book of Chanokh

CHANOKH

The Astronomical Book — The Dream Visions — DSS Attested

Chapters 37 through 55

The Solar Calendar of 364 Days — The Gates of the Sun and Moon — Chanokh Transmits to Metushelakh — The First Dream — The Flood Foreseen — The Animal Apocalypse — From Adam to the Messianic Age

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

Dead Sea Scrolls — 4QEnastr (4Q208-211) — Astronomical Book — Aramaic Primary

4QEnc-e (4Q204-206) · 4QEng (4Q212) — Dream Visions — DSS Confirmed

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

THE ASTRONOMICAL BOOK — CHAPTERS 37 THROUGH 47

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 37 The Sun — Its Gates and Its Circuit

1 The book of the courses of the luminaries of the heaven, the relations of each, according to their classes, their dominion and their seasons, according to their names and places of origin, and according to their months, which Uriel, the holy angel who was with me and who is their guide, showed to me — and he showed me all their laws exactly as they are, and how it is with regard to all the years of the world and unto eternity, till the new creation is accomplished which endureth till eternity.

2 And this is the first law of the luminaries: the luminary the Sun has its rising in the eastern portals of the heaven, and its setting in the western portals of the heaven.

3 And I saw six portals in which the sun rises, and six portals in which the sun sets and the moon rises and sets in these portals, and the leaders of the stars and those whom they lead — six in the east and six in the west, and all following each other in accurately corresponding order.

4 And there are many windows to the right and left of these portals, and one window at a certain season grows hot, that is those great portals out of which the sun goes forth, and in the windows blow a great heat when the sun goes forth from the portals.

5 And when the sun rises in the heaven he comes forth through that fourth portal thirty mornings in succession, and sets accurately in the fourth portal in the west of the heaven.

6 And through the fourth portal from which the sun rises in the first month are also thirty mornings, and he sets in the west also in the fourth portal.

7 At that season the day becomes longer from the night, and the day becomes longer and the night becomes shorter — both by day and by night.

8 And on that day the day is longer than the night by a ninth part, and the day amounts exactly to ten parts and the night to eight parts.

9 And the sun rises from the fourth portal and sets in the fourth portal and returns to the east, and rises thirty mornings in the third portal and sets in the west in the third portal.

10 And at the commencement of the morning with the rising of the sun through the eastern portals there is heat for thirty days.

11 On those days the day is shorter and the night is longer, and for thirty days each night amounts to exactly nine parts and the day to nine parts.

12 And at the commencement of the morning, when the sun rises, it rises through the first portal for thirty mornings and sets in the west in the first portal.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEnastr — four manuscripts 4Q208, 4Q209, 4Q210, 4Q211 — preserve extensive portions of the Astronomical Book in Aramaic. These are among the largest Chanokh fragments found at Qumran and demonstrate that the astronomical content was of primary interest to the Qumran community. The solar calendar described in the Astronomical Book is a 364-day calendar — 52 weeks exactly — which the Qumran community used as their covenant calendar, in opposition to the lunisolar calendar used by the Jerusalem Temple establishment. The calendar conflict between the Qumran community and the Temple authorities was a central theological dispute, and the Astronomical Book of Chanokh was their primary textual authority for the 364-day solar calendar.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 72 in full. The Ethiopian calendar tradition also preserves elements of the ancient solar reckoning.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 72:1 — the book of the courses of the luminaries, which Uriel the holy angel showed to Chanokh. Uriel — the angel of light — is specifically assigned as the guide for the Astronomical Book. He is the same angel who goes with Chanokh through portions of the cosmic journeys in the Book of the Watchers. His assignment to the astronomical sections confirms the thematic unity of the Chanokh corpus: the same being who guides Chanokh through the prisons of the fallen angels and the ends of the earth also guides him through the mechanics of the created luminaries. Light — natural and supernatural — is Uriel's domain throughout.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 38 The Moon — Its Phases and Its Gates

1 And after this law I saw another law dealing with the smaller luminary, which is named the Moon.

2 Her chariot which she rides in secret. And her circumference is like the circumference of heaven, and her chariot which she rides is driven by the wind, and light is given to her in definite measure.

3 And her rising and setting change every month, and her days are like the days of the sun, and when her light is uniform it amounts to the seventh part of the light of the sun.

4 And thus she rises. And her first phase in the east comes forth on the thirtieth morning, and on that day she becomes visible, and constitutes for you the first phase of the moon on the thirtieth day together with the sun in the portal where the sun rises.

5 And the one half of her goes forth by a seventh part, and her whole circumference is empty, without light, with the exception of one seventh part of it, the fourteenth part of her light.

6 And she sets with the sun, and when the sun rises the moon rises with him and receives the half of one light, and on that night in the beginning of her morning the moon sets with the sun, and is invisible that night with the fourteen parts and the half of one of them.

7 And she rises on that day with exactly a seventh part, and comes forth and recedes from the rising of the sun, and in her remaining days she becomes bright in the remaining thirteen parts.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEnastr (4Q208-211) preserves substantial portions of Chapter 73 in Aramaic. The lunar phase calculations are among the most technically detailed passages in the DSS Chanokh manuscripts.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 73 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] The Astronomical Book treats the moon as a secondary luminary that receives its light in measured portions from the sun. The lunar month is precisely 29.5 days — described here in terms of the moon's fourteen-part light cycle growing and diminishing. The relationship between the solar and lunar calendars is the central astronomical problem of the ancient covenant community: the 364-day solar calendar of Chanokh does not align with the 354-day lunar year, creating a tension that drove the calendar disputes between the Qumran community and the Jerusalem Temple authorities. The Book of Chanokh sides definitively with the solar calendar as the divinely revealed order.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 39 The Difference Between the Solar and Lunar Years

1 And I saw another course, a law for her, and how according to that law she performs her monthly revolution.

2 And all these Uriel, the holy angel who is the leader of them all, showed to me, and their positions, and I wrote down their positions as he showed them to me, and I wrote down their months as they were, and the appearance of their lights till fifteen days were accomplished.

3 In three years are one thousand and sixty two days, and in five years nine hundred and seventy seven days, so that in eight years there are seven thousand one hundred and thirty six days, adding the days that go beyond what is required, and adding five days.

4 And the year is accurately completed in conformity with their world-stations and the stations of the sun, which rise from the portals through which it rises and sets thirty days.

5 And the leaders of the heads of the thousands, who are placed over the whole creation and over all the stars, have also to do with the four intercalary days, being inseparable from their office, according to the reckoning of the year, and these render service on the four days which are not reckoned in the reckoning of the year.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEnastr (4Q208-211) preserves portions of Chapter 74. The intercalation mathematics are attested in the Cave 4 Astronomical Book fragments.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 74 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 74:12-14 — the mathematical reconciliation of solar and lunar year counts. The Astronomical Book preserves detailed numerical reckoning that tracks the divergence between the 364-day solar year and the lunar year across multiple year cycles. The four intercalary days — the extra days beyond the 52 complete weeks of the solar year — are governed by specific angelic leaders. The covenant calendar is not merely an administrative convenience — it is a divinely ordered system with angelic custodians maintaining its accuracy.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 40 The Intercalary Days — The Four Quarters of the Year

1 And the leaders of the heads of the thousands, who are placed over the whole creation and over all the stars, have also to do with the four intercalary days, being inseparable from their office, according to the reckoning of the year, and these render service on the four days which are not reckoned in the reckoning of the year.

2 And owing to them men go wrong therein, for those luminaries truly render service on the world-stations, one in the first portal, one in the third portal of the heaven, one in the fourth portal, and one in the sixth portal, and the exactness of the year is accomplished through its separate three hundred and sixty-four stations.

3 For the signs and the times and the years and the days the angel Uriel showed to me, whom YHWH of Glory has set for ever over all the luminaries of the heaven, in the heaven and in the world, that they should rule on the face of the heaven and be seen on the earth, and be leaders for the day and the night — the sun, moon, and stars, and all the ministering creatures which make their revolution in all the chariots of the heaven.

4 In like manner twelve doors Uriel showed me, open in the circumference of the sun's chariot in the heaven, through which the rays of the sun break forth, and from them is warmth diffused over the earth, when they are opened at their appointed seasons.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEnastr preserves portions of Chapter 75 in Aramaic.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 75 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 75:2 — owing to them men go wrong therein. The Astronomical Book explicitly states that the failure to observe the four intercalary days of the 364-day solar calendar causes humanity to err in their reckoning of times and seasons. Wrong calendar means wrong appointed times — wrong Sabbaths, wrong feasts, wrong new moons. For the Qumran community this was not an abstract astronomical dispute but a matter of covenant faithfulness. Celebrating YHWH's appointed times on the wrong days was, from their perspective, a fundamental covenant breach.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 41 The Twelve Winds and Their Portals

1 And at the ends of the earth I saw twelve portals open to all the quarters of the heaven, from which the winds go forth and blow over the earth.

2 Three of them are open on the face of the heavens, and three in the west, and three on the right of the heaven, and three on the left.

3 And the three first are those of the east, and three are of the north, and three after those on the left of the south, and three of the west.

4 Through four of these come winds of blessing and prosperity, and from those eight come hurtful winds — when they are sent, they bring destruction on all the earth and on the water upon it, and on all who dwell thereon, and on everything which is in the water and on the land.

5 And now I have shown thee, my son Metushelakh, all the laws of the luminaries and of the stars.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEnastr preserves portions of Chapter 76.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 76 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 76:14 — now I have shown you, my son Metushelakh. The Astronomical Book is addressed to Chanokh's son Metushelakh — the transmission of astronomical knowledge from father to son is the educational structure of this section. The covenant knowledge system in Chanokh is patrilineal: Chanokh receives from YHWH through Uriel and transmits to Metushelakh, who transmits to Lamekh, who transmits to Noakh. The astronomical calendar is part of this lineage of knowledge, passed down through the pre-flood patriarchs as covenant heritage.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 42 The Four Quarters of the Earth — The Seven Mountains and Seven Rivers

1 And the first quarter is called the east, because it is the first, and the second, the south, because the Most High will descend there, yea, there in quite a special sense will he who is blessed forever descend.

2 And the west quarter is named the diminished, because there all the luminaries of the heaven wane and go down.

3 And the fourth quarter, named the north, is divided into three parts, the first of them is for the dwelling of men, and the second contains seas of water, and the abysses and forests and rivers, darkness and clouds, and the third part contains the garden of righteousness.

4 I saw seven high mountains, higher than all the mountains which are on the earth, and thence comes forth hoarfrost, and days, seasons, and years pass away.

5 I saw seven rivers on the earth larger than all the rivers, one of them coming from the west pours its waters into the Great Sea.

6 And these two go towards the north to the sea and pour their waters into the Erythraean Sea in the east.

7 And the seventh river is Yarden, and it falls into and pours its waters into the Dead Sea.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEnastr preserves a portion of Chapter 77.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 77 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 77:8 — the seventh river is Yarden. The Astronomical Book's geographical summary identifies the Yarden — the Jordan — as one of the seven great rivers of the earth, flowing into the Dead Sea. The geographical knowledge embedded in the Astronomical Book places the covenant land — with the Yarden as its defining geographical feature — within the cosmic framework of the entire earth's river system. The covenant land is not a small provincial territory — it is mapped within a total cosmological geography.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 43 The Luminaries — The Names of the Sun and Moon

1 And the names of the sun are the following: the first Orjares, and the second Tomas.

2 And the moon has four names: the first name is Asona, the second Ebla, the third Benase, and the fourth Erae.

3 These are the two great luminaries — their circumference is like the circumference of the heaven, and the size of the circumference of both is alike.

4 In the circumference of the sun there are seven portions of light which are added to it more than to the moon, and in definite measures it is transferred till the seventh portion of the sun is exhausted.

5 And in certain months the month has twenty-nine days and once twenty-eight.

6 And Uriel showed me another law: when light is transferred to the moon, and on which side it is transferred to her from the sun.

7 During all the period during which the moon is growing in her light, she is transferring it to herself when opposite to the sun during fourteen days, and when she is illumined throughout, her light is accomplished full in the heaven.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEnastr (4Q208-211) preserves portions of Chapter 78 in Aramaic.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 78 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] The naming of the sun and moon — each luminary having multiple names — reflects the ancient understanding that names carry the nature and function of the thing named. The sun has two names corresponding to its two primary functions: giving light and giving heat. The moon has four names corresponding to its four phases. The naming system in the Astronomical Book is consistent with the broader Chanokh tradition in which YHWH names the stars individually and each star responds — Chapter 43. The covenant between YHWH and his creation is expressed through naming, just as the covenant between YHWH and the patriarchs is expressed through naming — Avram to Avraham, Yaakov to Yisra'EL.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 44 The Summary of the Astronomical Laws

1 And now, my son Metushelakh, I have shown you everything, and the law of all the stars of the heaven is completed.

2 And he showed me all the laws of these for every day, and for every season of bearing rule, and for every year, and for its going forth, and for the order prescribed to it every month and every week.

3 And the waning of the moon which takes place in the sixth portal, for in this sixth portal her light is accomplished, and after that there is the beginning of the waning.

4 And the waning which takes place in the first portal in its season, till one hundred and seventy-seven days are accomplished, reckoned according to weeks, twenty-five weeks and two days.

5 And so the year is accurately completed in conformity with their world-stations and the stations of the moon, and the stations of the sun, which rise and set in those portals, for thirty days each.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEnastr preserves portions of Chapter 79 in Aramaic.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 79 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Chapter 79 summarizes the entire Astronomical Book's teaching to Metushelakh. The covenant calendar rests on precise astronomical observation codified in the laws shown by Uriel. Verse 79:6 — the year is accurately completed in conformity with the stations of the sun and moon. The 364-day solar year, the lunar month calculations, and the intercalary days together constitute the complete calendar system that Chanokh received and transmitted. Any deviation from this system — any adoption of a different calendar — is, from the Chanokh tradition's perspective, a departure from the divinely revealed order of time.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 45 The Perversion of Nature in the Days of the Sinners

1 And in those days the angel Uriel answered and said to me: Behold, I have shown you everything, Chanokh, and I have revealed everything to you that you should see this sun and this moon, and the leaders of the stars of the heaven and all those who turn them, their tasks and times and departures.

2 And in the days of the sinners the years shall be shortened, and their seed shall be tardy on their lands and fields, and all things on the earth shall alter, and shall not appear in their time, and the rain shall be kept back and the heaven shall withhold it.

3 And in those times the fruits of the earth shall be backward, and shall not grow in their time, and the fruits of the trees shall be withheld in their time.

4 And the moon shall alter her order, and not appear at her time.

5 And in those days the sun shall be seen and he shall journey in the evening on the extremity of the great chariot in the west and shall shine more brightly than accords with the order of light.

6 And many chiefs of the stars shall transgress the order prescribed, and these shall alter their orbits and tasks, and not appear at the seasons prescribed to them.

7 And the whole order of the stars shall be concealed from the sinners, and the thoughts of those on the earth shall err concerning them, and they shall be altered from all their ways — yea, they shall err and take them to be deities.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEnastr preserves portions of Chapter 80 in Aramaic.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 80 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Chapter 80 is the Astronomical Book's prophetic counterpart to its scientific content. The precise astronomical order described in Chapters 72-79 will be disrupted in the days of the sinners. Rain withheld, harvests delayed, the moon altered, the stars transgressing their orbits, the sun shining outside its proper order. The disruption of the astronomical order is both the consequence of human sin and the sign that the days of judgment are approaching. Creation's faithfulness to its ordained order — the standard established in Chapters 2-5 of the Book of the Watchers — is undermined by the sinners' rebellion. Creation itself is affected by covenant breach.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 46 The Heavenly Tablets — Chanokh Reads the Fate of All Men

1 And he said unto me: Chanokh, look at the book of the tablets of heaven, and read what is written thereon, and mark every individual fact.

2 And I looked at everything in the tablets of heaven, and I read everything which was written thereon and understood everything, and read the book of all the deeds of men, and of all the children of flesh that shall be upon the earth to the remotest generations.

3 And forthwith I blessed the great Lord the King of Glory for ever, in that he has made all the works of the world, and I praised YHWH because of his patience, and blessed him because of the children of men.

4 And after that I said: Blessed is the man who dies in righteousness and goodness, concerning whom there is no book of unrighteousness written, and against whom no day of judgment shall be found.

5 And those seven holy ones brought me and placed me on the earth before the door of my house, and said to me: Declare everything to your son Metushelakh, and show to all your children that no flesh is righteous in the sight of YHWH, for he is their Creator.

6 For one year we will leave you with your son, till you give your last commands, that you may teach your children and record it for them, and testify to all your children — and in the second year they shall take you from their midst.

7 And now, my son, go and tell your son Metushelakh all these things, and show to all your children that no flesh is righteous before YHWH, for he is their Creator.

8 For I will leave you one year of peace with your son, that you may give commandment to your children regarding it, and record it for them, and testify to all your children — and in the second year they shall take you from their midst.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEng (4Q212) preserves portions of Chapter 81 in Aramaic.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 81 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 81:1-2 — the tablets of heaven contain the book of all the deeds of men and of all the children of flesh to the remotest generations. The heavenly tablets are the cosmic record of every human life and action. Chanokh reads it in its entirety. The same heavenly tablets appear in the Book of Yovelim — where Moshe is shown the tablets of heaven on Sinai — and in the War Scroll's theology of predestination. The heavenly tablets establish that no action in human history is unrecorded. YHWH's judgment at the end is not a reconstruction from incomplete evidence — it is the reading of a complete and perfect record.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 47 The Transmission to Metushelakh — The Seasons and Their Leaders

1 And now, my son Metushelakh, all these things I am recounting to you and writing down for you — I have revealed to you and given you the book concerning all these things. Preserve, my son Metushelakh, the books from your father's hand, and see that you deliver them to the generations of the world.

2 I have given wisdom to you and to your children, and your children that shall be to you, that they may give it to their children for generations — this wisdom namely that passes their understanding.

3 And those who understand it shall not sleep, but shall listen with the ear that they may learn this wisdom, and it shall please those that eat thereof better than good food.

4 Blessed are all the righteous, blessed are all those who walk in the way of righteousness and sin not as the sinners, in the reckoning of all their days in which the sun traverses the heaven, entering into and departing from the portals for thirty days with the heads of thousands of the order of the stars, together with the four which are intercalated which divide the four portions of the year, which lead them and enter with them four days.

5 And these are the names of those who lead them, who watch that they enter at their times, in their orders, in their seasons, in their months, in their periods of dominion, and in their positions.

6 Their four leaders who divide the four parts of the year enter first, and after them the twelve leaders of the orders who divide the months — and for the three hundred and sixty there are heads over thousands who divide the days, and for the four intercalary days there are the leaders which sunder the four parts of the year.

7 And the name of the one is Melkiel, who is named Tame'el and the sun, and the name of all those whom he leads with him are Hesfael, Keel, Peeel, and the name of their head is Kerael.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEng (4Q212) preserves portions of Chapter 82 in Aramaic. The transmission command — preserve the books and deliver them to the generations of the world — is attested in the Cave 4 manuscripts.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 82 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 82:1 — preserve, my son Metushelakh, the books from your father's hand, and deliver them to the generations of the world. Chanokh explicitly commands that his written books — the tablets, the astronomical records, the accounts of his visions — be preserved and transmitted across all generations. This is the Book of Chanokh's own account of how it survived the Flood and reached later generations: Metushelakh preserved it, transmitted it to Lamekh, Lamekh to Noakh, Noakh through the Flood. The chain of transmission is the covenant people's responsibility. The Qumran community's preservation of the Chanokh manuscripts in clay jars in the desert caves is the most recent link in a chain they understood to go back to Chanokh himself.

THE DREAM VISIONS — CHAPTERS 48 THROUGH 55

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 48 The First Dream Vision — The Flood Foreseen

1 And now, my son Metushelakh, I will show you all my visions which I have seen, recounting them before you.

2 Two visions I saw before I took a wife, and the one was quite unlike the other — the first when I was learning to write, the second before I took your mother, when I saw a terrible vision.

3 And regarding them I prayed to YHWH.

4 I had laid me down in the house of my grandfather Mahalale'el, when I saw in a vision how the heaven collapsed and was borne off and fell to the earth.

5 And when it fell to the earth I saw how the earth was swallowed up in a great abyss, and mountains were suspended on mountains, and hills sank down on hills, and high trees were rent from their stems, and hurled down and sunk in the abyss.

6 And thereupon a word fell into my mouth, and I lifted up my voice to cry aloud, and said: The earth is destroyed.

7 And my grandfather Mahalale'el waked me as I lay near him, and said unto me: Why do you cry so, my son, and why do you make such lamentation?

8 And I recounted to him the whole vision which I had seen, and he said unto me: A terrible thing have you seen, my dear son, and of grave moment is your dream-vision as to the secrets of all the sin of the earth — it must sink into the abyss and be destroyed with a great destruction.

9 And now, my son, arise and make petition to the Lord of Glory, since you are a believer, that a remnant may remain on the earth, and that he may not destroy the whole earth.

10 My son, from heaven all this will come upon the earth, and upon the earth there will be great destruction.

11 After that I arose and prayed and implored and besought, and wrote down my prayer for the generations of the world, and I will show everything to you, my son Metushelakh.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEnc (4Q204) preserves portions of Chapter 83 in Aramaic.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 83 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Chapter 83 introduces the second major section of the Dream Visions — the account of Chanokh's two dreams told to his son Metushelakh. The first dream foresees the Flood as a cosmic catastrophe — heaven collapses, earth is swallowed in the abyss, trees are torn up. Chanokh's grandfather Mahalale'el interprets the dream as showing the destruction that will come because of human sin. Chanokh prays for a remnant — and the prayer is answered in the covenant with Noakh. The visionary genealogy of the Flood narrative in Chanokh connects to the Bereshit account at every point: Chanokh sees it, Metushelakh preserves the account, Lamekh is warned through Uriel in Chapter 10, and Noakh builds the ark.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 49 The Prayer After the First Dream — YHWH's Sovereignty Declared

1 Then I fell on my face and my whole body melted, and my spirit was transfigured, and I cried with a loud voice, with the spirit of power, and blessed and glorified and extolled.

2 And these blessings which went forth from my mouth were well pleasing before that Head of Days.

3 And that Head of Days came with Mikha'el and Gavri'el, Rafa'el and Fanuel, thousands and ten thousands of angels without number.

4 And that angel came to me and greeted me with his voice, and said unto me: You are the man of faith to whom belongeth the word, and the light.

5 And when he came and greeted me with his voice, I fell before him and worshipped, and he blessed me, and said: Fear not, Chanokh, you righteous man and scribe of righteousness — approach hither and hear my voice.

6 Go, say to the Watchers of heaven, who have sent you to intercede for them: You should intercede for men, and not men for you.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEnc (4Q204) preserves portions of Chapter 84 in Aramaic.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 84 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 84:5 — fear not, Chanokh, you righteous man and scribe of righteousness — approach hither and hear my voice. YHWH's direct address to Chanokh in the first dream prayer mirrors his address in the throne vision of Chapter 14. The same title — scribe of righteousness — and the same command — fear not, approach and hear — mark both encounters. Chanokh's covenant identity is consistent across all sections of the book: he is always the righteous scribe who approaches YHWH at YHWH's invitation and receives what he could not have sought on his own initiative.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 50 The Second Dream Vision — The Animal Apocalypse Begins — Adam and Eve as a Bull and Heifer

1 And after this I saw another dream, and I will show the whole dream to you, my son.

2 And Chanokh lifted up his voice and spake to his son Metushelakh: To you, my son, will I speak — hear my words — incline your ear to the dream-vision of your father.

3 Before I took your mother Edna, I saw in a vision on my bed, and behold a bull came forth from the earth, and that bull was white, and after it came forth a heifer, and along with this latter came forth two bulls, one of them black and the other red.

4 And that black bull gored the red one and pursued him over the earth, and thereupon I could no longer see that red bull.

5 But that black bull grew and that heifer went with him, and I saw that many oxen proceeded from him which resembled and followed him.

6 And that cow, that first one, went from the presence of that first bull in order to seek the red one, but found him not, and lamented with a great lamentation over him and sought him.

7 And I looked till that first bull came to her and quieted her, and from that time onward she cried no more.

8 And after that she bore another white bull, and after him she bore many bulls and black cows.

9 And I saw in my sleep that white bull likewise grow and become a great white bull, and from him proceeded many white bulls, and they resembled him.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEnd (4Q205) and 4QEne (4Q206) preserve portions of Chapter 85 in Aramaic.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 85 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Chapter 85 opens the Animal Apocalypse — one of the most extensive allegorical historical narratives in ancient Hebrew literature. Human beings and angelic beings are represented as animals throughout, with the covenant people consistently represented as cattle and sheep, while their enemies are represented as birds of prey and wild beasts. The white bull of verse 85:3 is Adam, the heifer is Chavah, the black bull is Qa'in, the red bull is Hevel. Qa'in's murder of Hevel is the goring of the red bull. The entire history of humanity from Adam to the Maccabean period is encoded in this extended animal allegory.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 51 The Fallen Stars — The Watchers as Stars Descending

1 And again I saw with mine eyes as I slept, and I saw the heaven above, and behold a star fell from heaven, and it arose and ate and pastured among those oxen.

2 And after that I saw the large and the black oxen, and behold they all changed their stalls and pastures and their cattle, and began to live with each other.

3 And again I saw in the vision, and looked towards the heaven, and behold I saw many stars descend and cast themselves down from heaven to that first star, and they became bulls among those cattle and pastured with them among them.

4 And I looked at them and saw, and behold they all let out their privy members, like horses, and began to cover the cows of the oxen, and they all became pregnant and bare elephants, camels, and asses.

5 And all the oxen feared them and were affrighted at them, and began to bite with their teeth and to devour, and to gore with their horns.

6 And they began, moreover, to devour those oxen, and behold all the children of the earth began to tremble and quake before them and to flee from them.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEnd (4Q205) preserves portions of Chapter 86 in Aramaic.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 86 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] The falling stars of Chapter 86 are the Watchers — the same beings described in Chapter 6 as descending on Mount Hermon. In the Animal Apocalypse's symbolic language, the Watchers appear as stars that fall and become bulls among the cattle of humanity. Their union with the daughters of men — cows in the allegory — produces elephants, camels, and asses — the Nephilim rendered as the largest and most powerful non-human animals in the ancient world's experience. The fear of the covenant people before these monstrous offspring is shown in the oxen trembling and fleeing. The allegory and the direct narrative of the Book of the Watchers tell the same story in two different registers.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 52 The Four Men — The Archangels Descend

1 And I saw one of those four who had come forth first, and he seized that first star which had fallen from the heaven, and bound it hand and foot and cast it into an abyss — now that abyss was narrow and deep and horrible and dark.

2 And one of them drew a sword, and gave it to those elephants and camels and asses, then they began to smite each other, and the whole earth quaked because of them and trembled.

3 And as I was beholding in the vision, lo, one of those four who had come forth stoned them from heaven, and gathered and took all the great stars whose privy members were like those of horses, and bound them all hand and foot, and cast them into an abyss of the earth.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEnd (4Q205) preserves portions of Chapter 87 in Aramaic.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 87 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] The four men who descend to execute judgment in Chapter 87 are the four archangels of Chapter 9 — Mikha'el, Uriel, Rafa'el, and Gavri'el — translated into the animal allegory's symbolic language as unnamed men who arrive from heaven. Their actions in the allegory precisely parallel their assignments in Chapter 10: binding the fallen Watchers, casting them into the abyss, equipping the Nephilim to destroy each other. The Animal Apocalypse is not a separate tradition — it is the same theological material retold in visionary-allegorical form.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 53 The Flood in the Animal Apocalypse — Noakh as the White Bull

1 And one of those four went to that white bull and instructed him in a secret, without his being terrified — he was born a bull and became a man, and built for himself a great vessel and dwelt thereon, and three bulls dwelt with him in that vessel and they were covered in.

2 And again I raised mine eyes towards heaven and saw a lofty roof, with seven water torrents thereon, and those torrents flowed with much water into an enclosure.

3 And I saw again, and behold fountains were opened on the surface of that great enclosure, and that water began to swell and rise upon the surface, and I saw that enclosure till all its surface was covered with water.

4 And the water, the darkness, and mist increased upon it, and as I looked at the height of that water, that water had risen above the height of that enclosure, and was streaming over that enclosure, and it stood upon the earth.

5 And all the cattle of that enclosure were gathered together until I saw how they sank and were swallowed up and perished in that water.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEne (4Q206) preserves portions of Chapter 88 in Aramaic.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 88 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 88:1 — the white bull was born a bull and became a man, and built a great vessel. Noakh in the Animal Apocalypse is the white bull who is transformed into a man to build the ark. The transformation from animal to human form in the allegory marks the transition from one covenant epoch to another. White animals consistently represent the covenant line — Adam was white, Sheth was white, Noakh is white. The covenant lineage in the Animal Apocalypse is a line of white animals that persists through the darkness of the Nephilim epoch, the Flood, and into the patriarchal period.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 54 The Animal Apocalypse Continued — From Noakh to the Babylonian Exile

1 And that white bull which had become a man came out of that vessel, and the three bulls with him, and one of those three was white like that bull, and one of them was red as blood, and one black, and that white bull departed from them.

2 And they began to bring forth beasts of the field and birds, so that there arose different genera — lions, tigers, wolves, dogs, hyenas, wild boars, foxes, squirrels, swine, falcons, vultures, kites, eagles, and ravens.

3 And I saw till a great sword was given to the sheep, and the sheep proceeded against all the beasts of the field to slay them, and all the beasts and the birds of the heaven fled before their face.

4 And I saw that man, who wrote the book according to the command of YHWH, till he opened that book concerning the destruction which those twelve sheep had wrought, and read aloud before YHWH of the sheep.

5 And I saw till those sheep which had gone from the tower and had taken a journey to the distant land, I saw how they entered into a great river, and the river became dry and the land was dry, and all the beasts and all the birds of the sky stood on that dry ground.

6 And I saw till they came to a fine water, I saw till those sheep passed over that water, and that all the sheep which were with them, those which were not drowned, gathered themselves to a certain place.

7 And I saw in the vision how the ravens flew upon those lambs and took one of those lambs, and dashed the sheep in pieces and devoured them.

8 And the sheep were destroyed and dispersed, and they withdrew and hid themselves.

9 And I saw how YHWH of the sheep came to them and took in his hand the staff of his wrath, and smote the earth, and the earth clave asunder, and all the beasts and all the birds of the sky fell from among those sheep, and were swallowed up in the earth and it covered them.

10 And I kept seeing till those sheep came to a good place, and a pleasant and glorious land, and I saw till those sheep were satisfied, and that house stood among them in the pleasant land.

11 And in the vision I saw the ravens coming, and they came upon those sheep, and they fell upon them and devoured them.

12 And I kept seeing till that shepherd came to them again who cared for those sheep, and he begat and came to sleep and rested and awoke.

13 And I saw till the Lord of the sheep brought a new house greater and loftier than that first one, and set it up in the place of the first which had been folded up.

14 And I saw that their eyes were opened, and they saw well, and I saw that those sheep carried out all the tasks which YHWH of the sheep had commanded them, and they served him and followed his commandments.

15 And I kept seeing till those shepherds came in their appointed seasons, and began to deliver over those sheep to certain horsemen, and those horsemen and their armies came to those sheep, and there arose a great conflict between them.

16 And I saw that man, who wrote the book at the command of YHWH, he opened that book concerning the destruction which those twelve shepherds had wrought, and showed that they had destroyed much more than their predecessors, before YHWH of the sheep.

17 And I saw till those shepherds and eagles and those vultures and kites assembled and brought to that house those sheep, and began to smite those sheep, and they did not restrain themselves.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEne (4Q206) and 4QEng (4Q212) preserve portions of Chapter 89 in Aramaic. Chapter 89 is one of the longest chapters in the Book of Chanokh and covers the entire history of Yisra'EL from Noakh through the Babylonian exile.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 89 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Chapter 89 is the most extensive single chapter in the Animal Apocalypse and covers the entire sweep of Yisra'ELi history in allegorical form. The white bull Noakh gives birth to three sons — Shem the white, Ham the red or brown, and Yefet the black. The nations descended from Noakh's sons are the wild animals. The sheep — Avraham's descendants — emerge as the covenant people. Moshe is the man who writes the book at YHWH's command. The crossing of the sea — verse 89:16 — and the wilderness journey are described in the sheep allegory. The entry into the land, the period of the judges, the monarchy, the division of the kingdom, and the Babylonian exile all appear in coded form. The seventy shepherds who are given charge of the sheep during the exile period — the nations given authority over Yisra'EL — each receive their commission from YHWH and will be judged for exceeding it.

CHANOKH — CHAPTER 55 The Animal Apocalypse Concluded — The White Bull and the Messianic Age

1 And I saw till that in this respect the thirty-five shepherds undertook the pasturing of the sheep, and completed their periods as did the first, each in his own period.

2 And I saw till the Lord of the sheep came unto them in wrath, and all who saw him fled, and they all fell into his shadow from before his face.

3 And I saw one of those four who had come forth first, and he seized that first star again which had fallen from the heaven, and he bound it hand and foot and cast it into an abyss.

4 And all the eagles and vultures and ravens and kites were still assembled, and there came with them all the sheep of the field — they all came together, and helped each other to break that horn of the ram.

5 And I saw till a throne was erected in the pleasant land, and the Lord of the sheep sat himself thereon, and the other took the sealed books and opened those books before the Lord of the sheep.

6 And judgment was held first over the stars, and they were judged and found guilty, and went to the place of condemnation, and they were cast into an abyss, full of fire and flaming, and full of pillars of fire.

7 And those seventy shepherds were judged and found guilty, and they were cast into that fiery abyss.

8 And I saw at that time how a like abyss was opened in the midst of the earth, full of fire, and they brought those blinded sheep, and they were all judged and found guilty and cast into this fiery abyss, and they burned.

9 And I saw till the Lord of the sheep brought a new house, and he erected it in the place of the first house which had been folded up — all the pillars were new, and its ornaments were new and larger than those of the first, the old one which he had taken away, and all the sheep were within it.

10 And I saw all the sheep which had been left, and all the beasts on the earth, and all the birds of the heaven, falling down and doing homage to those sheep and making petition to and obeying them in every thing.

11 And I saw that a white bull was born, with large horns, and all the beasts of the field and all the birds of the air feared him and made petition to him all the time.

12 And I saw till all their generations were transformed, and they all became white bulls — and the first among them became a lamb, and that lamb became a great animal and had great black horns on its head, and YHWH of the sheep rejoiced over it and over all the oxen.

13 And I slept in their midst, and I awoke and saw everything.

14 This is the vision which I saw while I slept, and I awoke and blessed YHWH of righteousness and gave him glory.

15 Then I wept with a great weeping and my tears stayed not till I could no longer endure it — when I saw, they flowed on account of what I had seen, for everything shall come and be fulfilled, and all the deeds of men in their order were shown to me.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QEng (4Q212) preserves portions of Chapter 90 in Aramaic.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 90 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT]

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 90:37-38 — a white bull was born with large horns, all beasts feared him, and the first among them became a lamb, and that lamb became a great animal. The Animal Apocalypse ends with the eschatological age inaugurated by the appearance of a white bull — the restoration of the covenant line to the original white-bull form of Adam and Noakh. The first of the transformed becomes a lamb that grows into a great animal. The allegory closes the entire history of humanity — from Adam the white bull through all the violence and exile of human history — with a return to the original form. All generations are transformed into white bulls. YHWH of the sheep rejoices. Chanokh weeps — not from sorrow but from the overwhelming weight of what he has seen.
